speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, you know, my father—am I right, the way I'm talking?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: My father, you pretty much always had to ask him for money. He didn't just—but you could charge and he'd pay the bills. But like we youngsters, of course I was the last one, and if anyone would have had an allowance, I'd been the one because he had liberalized his way of thinking about his family so much. My brother, who was five—Morris, who was five years older than I. We did so many things that our older brothers and sisters couldn't do. We weren't told, you have to be home at 1030 from a party. I was never told that in my life, but we didn't go and stay on earthly hours or anything. But my older brother and sisters, even if they went to a dance, they had to come home at 1030. Of course, the dances started earlier, but that was just the rule of the house. But as far as money was concerned,

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: there wasn't a lot of it, but we had to ask. And I think I showed you a check the other day my father had written on. He was the one that had one. But he never said, you can't have it, but you had to ask for it and sort of say what you were going to do with it. But with running the house, my mother was never restricted as to what she could buy or anything. But she just—of course, once every year when Creighton's had their red tag sale, she would go in and buy so many sheets. Well, she made the sheets, you see. She brought the muslin and made the sheets way back when I was a child. And so many white tablecloths and like yardage. And then I think it wasn't charged on a monthly basis. It was probably paid twice a year, the account at Creighton's store. And as far as the groceries were concerned, we had—the man would come around to our door each morning to see what we wanted. We always had a charge account. And Mama would order what she needed and they would deliver it later in the day. But of course, you didn't need to go in because there were no fresh vegetables or anything. You didn't need to go in to see what you wanted. It was just a matter of staples and things like that that you ordered. Because if you had the vegetables you had, they were canned and in your own cellar rather than by being in bins at the store.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But my dad every night would spend—he'd read the evening papers after we'd had supper. And he'd read the papers, the daily papers. And then he'd go to his desk, which was in Papa's and Mama's bedroom, and sit at his desk as long as it took him to account for all the day's spending and put it in a book, in a ledger. And that was done every day. That was never—and he knew exactly the amount of money that had been spent or that had been given to his child, or if he'd given Mama any cash. I know in that book that I'm going to let you see at register, wife, two dollars. Well, now I don't have no idea what Mama wanted to spend two dollars for, but that was the way it was recorded in the book, wife, two dollars. And as far as our schoolbooks and school supplies, Hodgins were the only ones who had school books in those days. And we got what we needed and charged it, and my dad would pay the bill. And we weren't extravagant because, I mean, buying junk and stuff we didn't need, we knew we should get what we needed and that was it. And my mother's health wasn't very good. When I was a little child about eighteen months or two years old, she was taking my oldest sister out to visit friends in the country. And some boys came along on horseback wearing chaps, what they call it, chaps, and yelling and shouting and having fun. And it frightened the horse and he ran away. And he went off of the road down an incline. And I don't remember this, of course, but it broke my mother's collarbone and somebody brought her into town and I guess got the horse and the buggy and so on.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I had wanted to go so bad with Mama that morning, but she left me at home with my other sisters. And it was very fortunate that she didn't have the baby sitting in her lap or something while she was driving. But the way they—and I think it must have been Dr. Watkins, I don't remember. But the next door neighbor came over to help when the doctor came and he had her make out of muslin and an old sheet or something. Two big donuts stitched them together and my mother's arms were put through the holes. They stuffed them with cotton. And my mother's arms were put through the holes and they laced them together front and back to hold that collarbone in place. Well, as a result, it mended across at the end. It didn't heal directly the two parts of the bones together. One was on top of the other. And she always, always had aches and pains in that collarbone, you know, damp weather or anything. But it must have been neuritis. It probably pressed on a nerve or something. And so years later, I remember this, Dr. Gritman did an operation on Mama's collarbone and sawed the bones apart and wired them together. And she was that way the rest of her life with her collarbone wired together. But she was never, from the time I was about, oh, I judge, I was eight or ten, she had to have some very severe surgery. I think it was for a kinked bowel, but a kinked colon. But it was very serious surgery and they despaired of her life. But my mother always went to prayer meeting every Wednesday or Thursday night, whenever it was at our church, they had the midweek prayer meeting. And so that particular night, they turned it into a prayer service for Mama. And she finally recovered. But it was such a big incision they made in her abdomen that she was never strong again. And so I remember my father got her—it was bigger than my little girl rocker, just a neck -sized rocker. And she would sit in that because it was hard for her to sit in an ordinary rocking chair. And she had wore what we called dressing sacks. They were like our house robes now, only they were short and she wore a skirt underneath. But her abdomen was quite big always after that because of the surgery. I suppose the muscles never recovered from such a big incision. And so she always wore a dressing sack. But some dressmakers, we usually had our clothes made at home, but a dressmaker made her a fancy one. And I can remember it was wool, light -weight wool, gray, and it was tied with a ribbon around the waist and had a fancy collar. So that was sort of her dress -up clothes while she was recuperating from her surgery. So my mother never could do heavy housework or anything after that. And after everybody had their chores to do in our home—

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I do not remember so much, only I always had to wipe dishes because we wiped them then, you know. And I can remember having to wipe dishes after supper. And my oldest brother was in the Philippines then because he graduated from college in 1901. And he took his degree in absentia because he was sent to the Philippines. And I have a picture of Albert telling Mama goodbye out on the front door porch. And she had this dressing sack on that I have told you about. And Albert stood there in his overcoat and with his suitcase. And he had for some reason had gotten a wide -brimmed hat like—well, we always thought of Teddy Roosevelt wearing them, you know. I do not know why. It probably was a Stetson, a good hat or something. But he was sent to the Philippines by the United States government. He was a civil engineer. And he had charge of lighthouse and bridge construction in the Philippines. And it was considered a very fine job. And it was, I think, President Taft was president then. And they had a great deal of social life in Manila. And my brother kept polo, polonies, you know. Their houses were built up and the animals were in the below. But they were built up for—well, it was healthier to live up off of the ground because I do not know whether it was swampy ground or what it was. But Harry and I visited Manila in 1963 on a trip to the Orient. And we were at three to four weeks' voyage to get there, you see. And when my brother, older than I, Morris, who was five years older than I am, he was in prep school at the university. Well, he was at the age he just did not think he was getting any place. And my father was pretty lenient with Morris like he was with me. And Albert wrote that if Morris wanted to come over there, he knew he could get a job. And so Morris, I think he was eighteen or nineteen. He had not finished prep school. And he went over there. My father let him go over there. And he lived with Albert in his home over there. And of course, they kept Filipino boys to do their work. And Morris really just thought that was the height of perfection to have a Filipino boy come and hand him a book or his cigarettes or something, whatever he wanted to be weighted on. He just ate it up. And Albert had told him, you had to have your white clothes all made to measure. They wore whites entirely because of the heat, white ducks and so on. I think they were all duck material. And so he told Morris he could go to his tailor and have him make what Morris thought he needed. Well Morris liked clothes awfully well. So he went all out. He had everything. He had just everything he could use. And then after he had been there a little while, he got a job with the Manila General Hospital, which was a huge hospital, as chief clerk. That was who kept the books and the accounts and so on. So he did all right for himself, even though he was very young. And of course there was a great deal of social life, which he enjoyed. But when he was—and my oldest brother Albert then had to come home in 1908 because his health was getting bad, the climate and his work and all. And even before he could come home, he went to some health clinic on the coast or some place, or San Francisco, I don't know where, because I just was young enough that I didn't pay much attention to build himself up a little before he ever came home. But he arrived home in time for my sister Ethel's wedding, which was in December of 1908. But in 1911, Morris, who was still over there and working as chief clerk in the Manila General Hospital, became very ill and had to have surgery. And it was evidently a gallbladder operation. Well, my mother and my father didn't know about this surgery ahead of time, but they wrote—somebody wrote in the meantime that he was in the hospital and had had surgery. But in February of 1911, the cablegram came to my brother Albert, who was living in Tacoma then. He had taken a job with a big construction company. In fact, he built that big famous depot in Tacoma that has been made a national landmark or shrine, national monument. And the city of Tacoma is keeping it, although it isn't in use as a station anymore. But it's a beautiful building with a big dome on top. It's beautiful. Whenever I've gone there, I always admire it with great pride, because my brother built that, the company he was with. But this cablegram came to Albert that Morris had died of septicemia, which was blood poisoning, of course. And they were to have a funeral service there in Manila, and then his body was to be sent home. So about the middle of March, his body arrived in San Francisco, and my brother and my father went to bring it home. And he was buried in the Moscow cemetery at the age of 22, which was pretty sad. And my mother never quite got over that. It was just a bop broke her heart.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was Morris sort of her favorite?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, no. Because he was young. He was just her boy. Of course,

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: when we were kids, we always said, Mama favored the boys in the family, and Dad favored the girls. You know how kids are. But to Mama, her boys were just perfect, and they were good to her. But my brother Albert, my two sisters were in college, and he was so good, he sent them money every year. And they bought nice material and had a new ball gown every year, a party dress, because they were in college and maybe Dad could not always afford it. And having two in college at the same time was expensive in those days, for their family income as it is now. But Albert always did that for the girls every year, and I know they were so proud of the dresses that they had that Albert had sent them the money to buy the material to make their party dresses.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that unusual for women to go to college?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, no. No. Most every girl that—oh, not every girl, but many Moscow girls went on to college. And of course, by that time, the Ridenbaugh Hall had been built. So they had a dormitory for women, although they had no accommodations for students on the campus, men students. But there were quite a few girls who had—well, many Moscow girls went on to college. Of course, that was the thing in our family. Everybody was supposed to go to college, although my oldest sister, Mabel, did not finish college.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And this is a long story.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Shall I tell it? Do you want to shut off a minute?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I suppose the boys went through a time when they didn't like girls. Of course, I know my brother Morris, when I was—he was five years older. And when I was about ten and he was fifteen, he thought I was the scum of the earth, you know. And I used to resent it that the boys could go out to the sand pit and swim, and little girls never could do things like that. And the boys would go on hikes and girls couldn't—of course, I was enough younger than he. He didn't—my mama wouldn't have let me go because I would have been a responsibility for him. But it always seemed like every time he went on one of those hikes that the boys used to have, something always happened to him. Whether he was accident -prone or what, I don't know. But my dad had given him—so this shows how lenient he was—a twenty -two for Christmas. And one time Morris had taken his twenty -two, which any mother would worry about going out with several boys. And some way, whether he was walking along or what, I don't know, but he got shot through the calf of his leg. That was one accident. And then another time, he was always collecting. Wherever he went, he was collecting something. And on this hike, he was looking for some special kind of rocks. And some boy in the group threw a rock against a tree, a live, small tree, and it bounded right back and hit Morris in the mouth and knocked out his two front teeth, his upper teeth. So I can just imagine my mother hating to have a Saturday come when the boys wanted to take a hike for fear something would happen to Morris. But I don't think she ever deprived him of going. But those were two things I remember that happened to him on these hikes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But— Kelly, did you play outside much?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: In the winter, we did lots of coasting. I can remember I always stayed too long and my hands would get so cold and my mother netted my mittens. She made me new ones every winter, whether they were worn out, whether I needed them or not. If the old pair—there was a real poor family who lived around the corner from us, and they had so many children, but they were proud. But she'd take those mittens out a little way from the sidewalk in front of our house and drop them down. And when they'd come along for school, they'd pick them up. And of course, they didn't know to whom they belonged, so they had a pair of mittens, because they were always bare -handed. And she used to feel so sorry. But I'd come home from coasting and I'd squall because my hands were so cold. Well, instead of putting them in warm water, as we think the thing is to do now, she'd put them in cold water and that made them hurt all the words. So I'd cry all the harder. But she was very patient. But we'd stay out too long. We were having such a good time that all at once we'd just be so cold we had to go home and get warmed up. But the snow was different then. It stayed on so much longer. Sometimes we had snow for Thanksgiving, but not always. But it would stay on for several months, as I remember. And then as I got a little older, we had sleigh ride parties and go to Pullman and have oyster stew or something over there, you know. Or go out in the country to some friend's house and have a supper. And that was a form of our winter social life, going on sleigh rides. And I remember once my sister Myra, who was home, chaperoned one of our sleigh ride parties, boys and girls. Of course, the man who drove the team had this big bed on the sleigh and he filled it with straw and we'd all take blankets. We were cozy and warm, but because my sister Myra was a chaperone, they thought she should sit up on the seat. And I know I had a muff, and it was in the style then of muff, they were kind of flat and there weren't a lot of filling in them. And so I insisted she take my muff. And when she practically froze to death, she held the muff up in front of her face, you know. I don't know that she ever chaperoned a party again for my group of friends because she just was entirely too uncomfortable on that sleigh ride party.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Laura? Did I?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We didn't, as far as boys and girls being together, I remember when we were around twelve and thirteen, we had a club. Oh, there must have been twenty -four, twenty -five children, boys and girls mixed. And we would have parties. Every once in a while somebody in the group would have a party. And we called our club the QAE Club.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Did I tell you this before?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, we better cut this out. Well, you've heard this.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: From poor households. Sometimes they just didn't have adequate warm clothing. But I don't know that there was ever any—we didn't have rummage sales. And maybe church organizations, women's aid societies and so on, ladies' aid societies in the churches did something to help out those people. But this family around the corner from us, the father was a blacksmith. And in the winter, I suppose, he didn't have too much to do. And we always considered that they were poor people. They didn't have as much as some of us did.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But of course, then, none of us had—telephones weren't too common. None of us had phones. Many of us had phones. We couldn't call each other up.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Only we had—did I tell you this?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: A neighbor who was a young lady in the family was one of the telephone operators. And they lived about two blocks from our house. So I used to go down to—their name was Ralph—to the Ralph home and call up Lillian Crithers, my friend, because her father was a doctor and they had a telephone. So I could call her up. I think she was about the only one of my friends who had a telephone. But I always thought that was pretty fine to be able to go to the neighbors and telephone.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And then another friend of mine—this is funny—and it was the Naylor family. They lived in town then, and Roy and Ralph and their sister Ruth lived out on the farm. They were through high school and lived out on the farm. And Mr. Naylor had an insurance business in Moscow, so they lived next door to Ralph's two blocks. And the youngest girl in the Naylor family, Nappa and I, were great friends. And they had a bathroom put on their house before we did. The sewer hadn't gone up as far as our house. And so Saturday morning Mrs. Naylor used to tell me, bring your clean clothes over and take a bath at our house. So I used to do that. And oh, it was fun to have a bath in a real bathtub. But of course it wasn't long after that. We had city water, of course, but the sewer came up Lynn Avenue, so my father built on the bathroom. And we had our own bathtub and toilet facilities right in the house then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you tell what you know of Wild Davey?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, when I was a little girl, we lived on Lynn Avenue, 722 Lynn Avenue. And Wild Davey lived—I don't think it was in the city limits, but it was down at the end of Lynn Avenue toward the bridge that crosses the creek down there on Highway 8. And a little house, and I think it must have had about two rooms in it. But when we'd go on picnics or anything and walk by there, of course a favorite place for us to take Sunday walks was to go to the cemetery and look at the monuments. And of course I was very interested because of my father. But we had to walk past Wild Davey's house, and we always went by pretty fast because we weren't actually afraid of him, but we just knew that was his home. And then he always had at least three dogs around. I don't remember even that the dogs barked at us, but we didn't tarry when we were passing Wild Davey's house. But he did go along Lynn Avenue, going to town with these three dogs. And I thought his hair was braided, but I see it was just flowing long hair in the picture. And I don't remember of him wearing a gun or anything as he had in the picture.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But he would pass our house, and of course if I was out in the yard playing or anything, why?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I always looked at Wild Davey as he passed by.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But did I cut into the experience out at Naylor's Farm?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Or was that just— Well, when I was a little girl then, at this time that Wild Davey lived down here, I was invited out to the Naylor Ranch every year for a week at two or three different times during the summer. He used to drop in at Naylor's every so often, and they'd invite him to eat at noon. He'd be hiking around the country. And they were always good to him. They gave him dinner. And Naylor and I, Ruth, the oldest sister, was living with her brothers and they were running the farm. And she was always scared we might be kind of smarty or something with him, and she always cautioned us. But then a favorite game of ours was to run through the rows of corn and pretend that Wild Davey was after us. And I don't know that he ever chased anybody in his life. I don't think that he was that kind of a person. But it was fun to imagine that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it just his looks and his dogs that were so interesting?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think so. And then he was very much to himself. I don't know that he ever—and how he happened to stop at Naylor's for his new meal. I don't know that. And the Naylor farm was four miles out of town or something. It probably was just a good morning's walk out there. But we were always told that whoever ran the hotels were very good to give him leftover food. And then Mr. Creighton, who owned Creighton's store, would give him clothing, we were told.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But as far as his ever doing anything, working at any job, I never knew of it if he did. But he was a character in Moscow, and we all accepted him as such. Nobody seemed to step over bounds and try to communicate with him or anything unless he wished it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he just leave town?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I don't actually know whatever happened. I can't remember how old I was or when we didn't know there was Wild Avian anymore. But finally somebody bought the house and moved it out on Orchard Avenue, where it still is, and rooms have been added to it. And then in the upper part of the land that his house occupied, people built a home. And it was afterwards—I can't remember who built it, I could if I thought about it. But afterwards, Mrs. Laney, that was Dr. and Mrs. Laney's home. He was on the faculty at the university. And that was on the land that Wild Avian had lived on.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that he had written books?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I don't think I have ever heard that Wild Avian wrote a book. But I know a man came here not too many years ago, it seems to me, getting information on Wild Avian. And I didn't know that Wild Avian had been with Kit Carson and so on. I just knew him as a child as Wild Avian in Moscow, and that was it. Of course, the children used to say that why he had so many dogs if he didn't have any food, he'd kill and cook one of these dogs. But I think that was just childhood speculation. I don't believe that was ever true, because I'm sure—I know the hotels were good to him to give him food.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Are there any other people that were at all like him that were unusual?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We did have a man here later, and they called him Seven Jackets. And it was because he actually wore seven jackets. I don't know whether he wore all the clothes he had, but he wasn't around very long. And I don't know where he lived or anything about what he did or anything, but he was called Seven Jackets, and he actually wore seven jackets. And whether he did it just because that was all the clothes he had or not, I don't know. But he wasn't around Moscow for very long. And of course, they were ex -Santrix, those two men. But we had some other people here who were—I used to wonder as a child, and I never asked for an explanation how they came to Moscow. And one was Sir John Moore.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did I tell you this?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But I don't know why he was called, but my parents always called him Sir John Moore. And whether he had come from England or what, I know where he lived in Moscow. But I don't know anything about him. I know that he was always known as Sir John Moore.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was he an English fellow?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I just rather imagine he was. And of course, you know about the Bovills.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And then there was— Did you meet them as a child? The Bovills?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes. I went with my family up there on a camping trip at Hog Meadows, I think. They expected the fish. And I was a small child on that camping trip, because my mother drove her horse and buggy. And I was the baby. And they left so early in the morning that I wasn't even dressed. I was carried out in my nightgown and wrapped in a blanket and put in the buggy. And I suppose when they stopped for lunch or something later on, I was dressed. But I don't remember too much about that camping trip, only that that was when Dr. Watkins was shot. And as we came back home on Sunday after the week's camping trip, out at the halfway stopping place where the Negro family, the black family lived, a man drove up with a team of horses and a buggy or a hack or something, and told the news about Dr. Watkins being shot that morning and what had gone on in Moscow. But by the time we got into the afternoon, which was real late afternoon, there was no evidence of it at the Steffen home.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Afterwards, we were told there were bullet holes in the house and whether I ever asked, they would have been on the other side anyway than the roadside on the road. Because the posse came down through the field and were in that. The creek was different in those days. They dredged it and made a new channel for it. But it did cross the Steffens' place and Paradise Creek. So that they were—it was summer, you see, and the posse was down. Perhaps the creek was dry or almost dry, and that was their barricade, the bank of the creek. But of course, as you know, the story was told that how the Steffens, young Steffens' mother came out and said, don't shoot anymore, he's dead. So the posse didn't—I don't know how they happened to see her, whether she carried a white something, so they didn't shoot or what. And there were supposed to—several in town claimed it was their bullet that killed him. I know there were two men in town that I know of that thought they were the ones who shot him. But maybe he killed himself, you don't know.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But that was an event in Moscow, of course, that will always be remembered, I presume, by old -timers. And of course, it's in Carol Rari Brink's book.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So that— I know Carol refers to a few, and she makes them sound like they were very ostracized people in town.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Right off. I can't think of any who that I actually knew who were divorced. Of course, divorce was very much out of line in those days, you know. And I just—I can't think of any. Of course, we had what they called the Red Light District. And of course, that was always a great source of curiosity amongst the youngsters in Moscow, because we knew the houses that they occupied down in the part of town where they were. And of course, Harry has some tales about delivering things down there to them.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: It was just all hearsay as far as we children were concerned. Of course, we were terribly curious about what they meant by a Red Light District and so on. You didn't know. Oh, no, we didn't realize what you were talking about. In those real early days, maybe before we were—my father and mother and the family came to Moscow before I was born. There was a great deal of gambling in Moscow, and for high stakes, too. But I don't know where the gambling took place or anything, because my father was not—if it was still going on after they came to town, my father was not a gambling man. So we never heard of who participated in these big high stake card games. But I think I'll tell you this. One day, my father was going to town and he walking along, he was going to work. He saw a ring and he picked it up, and it was a diamond ring.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Nobody ever put an ad—well, we did have a paper, but there was never any advertising for that ring or anything. And evidently, my father didn't run an ad that one had been found, because he just kept it for a while in safekeeping. And there was never, but we presumed that somebody had bet that diamond ring on cards. That was always what our presumption was in our family, and perhaps were intoxicated and lost it. But by and by, my father had my mother start wearing that ring. She had—no, she just had a wedding ring. She wore it, and that's been handed down in our family. I don't have it, but it still belongs in the family. It's an unusual mounting.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: My sister Myra wore it after my mother died. It was given to her. Then when she died, she wanted to go to a niece in the family, and it's there today.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We had three different schoolhouses. We had three during then, and none of them are in existence now. But my first two grades were in what we called the old high school on Third Street, which is where the educational unit is there now, across from the Methodist Church. Then my third grade was up in the old Russell School, which was a wooden structure, which was the first schoolhouse that was built in Moscow. But it had been—it was just a two -room school, one above and one below, a room below, when it was built. But they'd added a wing across the front with four rooms, two above and two below. I was in the lower room toward the north when I was in the third grade. Miss Clara Weatherit, an old family here in Moscow, was my school teacher. There were outside toilets. You had to go out of the building to go to the toilet.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think they were just a privy sort of a toilet at first. But then I went back to that building when I was in the fifth grade in an upstairs room in the original part of the building. By then, the toilets were outside, but they were the kind that when you sat on the water, the seat caused the water to come down from the tank or something. You didn't flush them yourself. They automatically flushed when you sat on the toilet seat. Then after that—my fourth grade, however, was up in Irving School, which was a wooden structure up on the corner of Adams and A Street, right on the corner.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think there were only four rooms in that building. As I remember, they were huge rooms, because the teachers carried a very great student load in those days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Levy - How big were your closets?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I think there were at least forty children when I was up there in my room in the fourth grade. Miss Lena Whitmore was my teacher. My sister, Myro, that was her first year of teaching. There was a little building down in that same block where the Russell School and the Irving School were that they called the Annex. She taught a fourth grade that year. There were lots of fourth graders, it seemed, in Moscow that year. She didn't have that many students, though, because the building was small. All of my friends went to my sister, but she didn't want her little sister in her room. I went to Miss Lena Whitmore, but Miss Whitmore was a lovely, lovely teacher. I enjoyed my year in her room. I remember so well, at the close of the year—I probably have it yet someplace—she gave us a little folder with her picture on the front. Maybe it was a program for the last day of school or something inside. Because we always had a program and spoke pieces and sang songs. Our mothers always came to the program. There were never any fathers because they were working. Our mothers always came to the last day of school for the afternoon program. I used to speak pieces. And then, as I told you before, I gave readings as I got older. That was my particular talent.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of things would you give as readings? What is the difference between a reading and a piece?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, a piece is usually poetry. That is what we considered then. When I graduated from eighth grade, we had graduation exercises just like high school. Of course, we just had a new white dress to wear for graduation. I read the class reading. I gave it that year. It was James Whitcomb Riley's The Bear Story. You probably don't know that. It was several pages long. But he sort of wrote free verse. That was the reading I gave. It was on the program as a reading. And then when I graduated from high school, I gave the class reading. That was a chapter from Mrs. Wiggs of the Cabbage Patch. I don't suppose you even heard of that book. I've heard of it. I've never read it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How were you picked to give the readings?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, just because you had spoken pieces all your life, I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did most people go on to high school from eighth grade?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Our eighth grade class was very big. I think there were two eighth grades. Probably all these fourth graders had come on to be eighth graders together. That was in the Irving School. We had the two upper rooms then, the eighth grade. And Mr. Margrethe, who was principal of the school, taught one.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I think they were—how was it?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't think there were any little children downstairs. Maybe the seventh grade. No, seventh grades were downstairs. It was departmentalized. We had different teachers come in for different subjects. You see, they'd come to that stage in our education. Before you go, I'll show you a picture of that eighth grade class. I've got that on stairs already to go to the museum. But the eighth grade classes were big because there were several families who had gone to country schools.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Their children—well, all the families hadn't moved to town because Arthur Eich was in the eighth grade, in my eighth grade, and his family still lived down on the farm. Whether the country schools quit having anything above the sixth grade then, I don't know the country schools surrounding Moscow. Because there was a girl, Clarice Abrahamson—they called her Clara, but her name was Clarice like mine—came in from out here at Blaine. I think she was driven back and forth by the family. Arthur Eich would have had to have been brought in school. I think there were others in that eighth grade who—Ward Gehno was in that class, but his family had moved in from the farm by the time he was in the eighth grade.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there any difference between the kids that came in from the country?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Not that I remember. Not that I remember there. They fitted right into the school program here.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: There were big classes and how the teachers could handle so many. But having it departmentalized, it made it easier with those big classes because probably one teacher taught English for seventh and eighth grades, you see. So that they actually didn't have to cover as much as if they taught all the—because I know one thing we had was agriculture. We had a little red book and just about farming and land in general, you know, that was what it was. It wasn't a very big book, but that was one subject that was taught in eighth grade agriculture. I don't know of anything else that was different. Of course, they weren't divided into social studies and scientific studies and so on, as they are now. But we had physiology and spelling and even writing. We had writing lessons and reading. And a separate course in grammar from the reading, because the reading was actually reading literature out of a book, you see. And you were asked to read orally. And I think that's why the children don't read so well today, not many of them, because they haven't had oral reading in school. But we did. And it was always a great pride if your mother came. And my mother would visit the school several times, visit my room several times during the year. Unannounced, of course, she'd knock on the door and teach her to open the door. And here was my mother come to visit the school. But many of the children's mothers did that. But if it happened to be reading class and you were called upon to walk up front and read, you were quite delighted. That was one of my better subjects, so it pleased me greatly to be asked to read when my mother was present. I read a great deal as a child to myself, books.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I had good—are you out of chance? Of course, I haven't told you anything about the—what are you hunting for?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't know. Quite often, some member of my family would give me a new book for my birthday or Christmas. But when I was a little girl—and this was before my oldest brother left home, because he used to tease me, so I read the Elsie Dinsmore books.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Have you ever heard of them?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, Elsie was a very—her father was away, and she seemed to live with her grandparents. This was a whole series of books you took Elsie through many years. But her father would come home to visit occasionally, and he expected such perfection in her.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And he would punish her if she didn't come up with his expectations. So my brother would tease me and say, well, come here, Elsie, father's going to spank you for not doing so and so. And of course Elsie would weep, and so I shed many tears along with Elsie. That was a whole series of books, Elsie Dinsmore books. Then a little later, the Five Little Pepper books came out. I have Five Little Peppers now, they grew, and Five Little Peppers abroad. Then of course we started—look how a little older I read Louise Alcott's books and so on progressed through my reading. When the library came to Moscow, they had a room up over where the—a part of Moscow or Brown's Furniture is now where they keep their appliances. Maybe you had this story told you about from someone. Well, every so often a big chest of books—and as I remember it was a big wooden chest—would come in from Boise with a new series of books, and you could borrow a book. The last—one of the books I got—I think the books were in Moscow for a month from this one chest load. Of course, you try to read several, you know. But the last book I got was quite a big book, and I didn't get to finish it and I had to return it. I don't know to this day what that book was, but I thought it was fascinating. But when I described it to people, they said—they have told me they bet it was Hans Brinker and the Silver Skates, because it was quite a thick book. And it may have been—I didn't get far enough into that I just don't know whether I was reading Hans Brinker. Of course, I read it afterwards, but I just can't associate what it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of books did you like to read?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Just children's stories. Of course, I can remember that from a child I always was interested in the newspapers. I didn't sit down and read the newspaper through, but there were certain things I loved to read as a little child in the newspaper when I could read. Then of course the magazines that came into the home, like the Ladies' Home Journal, which was like this then, and The Delineator, which was a style magazine and so on. Those things, even as a small child, I enjoyed.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And then when Carnegie gave the money for the city library, of course I started getting books there right away, because no home library had enough books to keep you supplied with reading if you were a reader in those days. So I started. You got a new card every so often, they checked your books out on a card. I don't remember what my first number was, but Mrs. McBride, her husband had been—now there is a case about a divorce—a dentist here. She became a librarian. I don't know whether she did before he died or not, but she was a librarian for years and years. Finally, whoever held number one card, my card ran out and she told me, Mrs. McBride told me, she said, you've read so many books in this library. I'm going to give you the number one card now. And so I held that number one card until they quit using that card file system. And I thought that was pretty nice. You want to shut it off just a minute? I'll let— What's your question about that?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How do people feel about him or about her? In the divorce?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yeah. Well, I never knew Dr. McBride personally. He wasn't our dentist. But she was a very intellectual sort of person. His first wife. His first wife. And I think she probably had a great deal to do with the historical club and study clubs in those days, because she was that type that liked that sort of thing. And I don't know why they were divorced, but he did marry this younger woman who became the city librarian. I can't remember her name. But he did. But as far as I can remember right off, that's the only actual divorce case I know of.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What you said reminds me of what you had to say about Mrs. Leadbrook.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you describe her?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, no, I really can't. She was a great friend of this Mrs. Headington, who boarded in our home when she came back— no, she hadn't been home. When she was in Moscow, after she gave up her own home, she was a widow and had lost her little girl. And she lived in our home for several years, was a boarder. And at that time, she was very active in the historical club, and I know Mrs. Leadbrook was too. And then later, Mrs. Leadbrook moved to Spokane, and Mrs. Headington used to go up to spend the weekend with her and so on, because they had such similar likes and enjoyed the same things so much. But I don't know anything about the Leadbrook home life more than the episode that Carol tells about. I didn't even know they were Methodists or anything, but Carol of course tells about the episode.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did that make much of an impression in Moscow?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I tell you, I don't know—of course, I wasn't any bigger than Carol was. Oh, yes, I was. No, not when that happened. Mrs. Leadbrook lived down here in Moscow for quite a few years. But I don't remember that there was ever much discussion in our home or amongst my friends about that. But of course, we didn't go to the Methodist Church, and we didn't know this family of Booths like the Methodist people, you see. And the Leadbrooks were also, evidently, he was a Methodist family.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: For most of your friends as a child, did most of them go to the Presbyterian Church also?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, it just happened. And so one or two didn't. When I was real small, one or two didn't. But my real close friends, because we were in the same Sunday school class. I remember when we were about ten, I judge, or maybe eight or nine or ten. The oldest Butterfield girl was married, and so she asked four of us to be flower girls. She taught our class in our Sunday school, but Butterfields were originally, I think, Episcopalians. But I know Ralston Butterfield was a great Episcopalian afterwards. But Mrs. Butterfield, the mother in her later years, she lived at Hotel Moscow, I believe. And she always came to our church. But anyway, this Miss Butterfield—and I can't say her name right off her first name—asked us to be flower girls at her wedding, and that was held in the Episcopal Church. And that wasn't this church that's here now, it was a wooden church. It burned up later. But there was a center aisle, and it wasn't very big, just as I remember about the size of the Episcopal Church as it is now. And I remember when we had the rehearsal the night before, Mrs. Butterfield, the mother of the bride, we were taking it so seriously to walk up the aisle two by two, just as we were supposed to do, and with our little baskets of rose petals. But we were so serious about it, I remember so well, she said to us, smile, children, smile. Because she didn't want us to be like that at the wedding, and she was trying to put us at ease, I think. She didn't want us to be little sourpusses at the wedding.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were those weddings big?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, that church wasn't too big, so I'm sure it was filled for that wedding. I think they had the reception up in the big Butterfield home, which is up on Polk Street.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Miss Butterfield used to have us tune her home for parties, her Sunday school class. And of course, none of us had come from big homes like that, and we just thought it was wonderful when she had us for a party.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember the first cars coming in?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes, I do. I think Dr. Crithers had the first car.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I can just remember of seeing it, but I never had a ride in it or anything. And then Dr. Gritman got a car. But then some friends of ours had moved in. It was the Brown family from the country, and they lived in a house built home just within the next block and around the corner from us. And their daughter and I were good friends. And somebody they knew had bought a car, and it was a front seat and then one little seat. It wasn't a rumble seat, it was just a little seat sitting behind it. You didn't lift up anything, the little seat was just there. So he was going to take my friend Alva Brown for a ride, this man. He was a friend of her father's. And so she asked me to go along, and I sat in the little seat behind. And oh, I thought it was marvelous to ride in a car. That's my first experience with cars. And then Ward Gano, when we were in high school, his father got a car. And I used to go places with Ward sometimes in the car. And the best ride is he'd fill a car with high school kids to go someplace.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember the first movies?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes, I do. And there were two movie houses here. And I think Mr. Kenworthy had one on one side of Third Street, and somebody else had one across the street. And we could go to the—we went to the show so often on a Saturday night, and they cost ten cents apiece for children. And if we had a quarter, we could go to both shows and have a bag of popcorn too, which we did. And I don't remember us going any other time of the week, only Saturday night. And I don't know. And then later on they started having matinees for children on Saturday afternoon. But I think that was when the theaters had moved down on Main Street, and that was when they started the matinees for children. And of course I was much—I thought I was much too big to go to a matinee then. But along the—when there was a theater on Main Street, we had a stock company in the summer. And they just lived here, you know, and I don't have no idea how they made enough money to keep them all on. But my sister Myra and I were at home. This was after nineteen and eight, because—oh well, I wouldn't have been going—I don't know if I was in high school or where I was. But we went to every change of program, which was every week. We just thought the stock company was wonderful. Oh, the printer always had sort of Wild West shows with a love story going in, because there was printer always shooting. I had to hold my ears, of course, because I didn't like the shooting. But oh, the man who played the leading man we thought was just the young girl of the town party was perfectly wonderful. I don't think the company consisted of more than four or five people. Sometimes they had to do a dual role, you know, to take all the parts. And the scenery was—I imagine if we'd see it now, we'd just think it was—they had backdrops, you know, with a city street on them. And of course the front curtain, before they'd raise it, was all these little ads of businesses in Moscow painted all over it, you see. I suppose. I don't know. Yes, we did have. They'd have printed the cast, the program printed of the cast. I can't remember the names of any of those, but they were quite some shows we thought.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When you went to prep school from high school, what was it that made you decide to go up there?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: What kind of kids went to— Well, I know one—no, she didn't be there. I don't believe she went to prep school. We just thought it was kind of this same group of girls that we'd been all through the grades together, my friend, three of us. And it was the three that we were always together. We just thought it was kind of smart, you know. We were just doing something different. We were at that age. But of course, I just went the one year the other two girls stayed on. They didn't live as far as I did, though. To get to an eight o 'clock class wasn't as hard for them, probably.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did many people come from the high school to the prep school?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Not too many. No, not too many. Most of them stayed.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have to have good grades or anything?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no. They were just glad to have the students in prep school, because the floor wasn't used for classes for the university. It did draw in from over the state young people where there weren't high schools, you know. And so, because I know in my class, Fred Theriault from Southern Idaho, he was a state legislator from down in Southern Idaho someplace for years afterwards. Oh no, that was Aidan Hyde. He came to prep school. And last I knew, he lost out on election. But then Fred Theriault came here from someplace, and I never would have gotten through physics if he hadn't helped me set up my apparatus every time. Then I remember a boy from down and around—I don't know where he was from, a country boy who came. And they all stayed down for college, you see. It was just a chance for them to get their high school and then go on to college.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it any harder than regular school?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no. Only you had—it was departmentalized, you had different teachers, of course. But we had that in high school, too. Only I think instead of the teachers changing room in high school, the pupils shifted to another room, because the halls weren't very big and there was always much confusion. Of course, we didn't mind that a bit at that age.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So you don't—prep school wasn't really that different?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no, no. It was just like high school. Only you were a little freer. You didn't have to sit in the study hall, you see, if you weren't having classes. We just always hung around in the hall and looked out toward the front of the building. From the third floor, we always saw everybody. Of course, college wasn't so big now. There were girls we admired very much and we'd watch for them, who we thought were very pretty.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think it was just a desire to be adult. Growing up, we figured it was more like going to college, you see. But then you went back to the high school for your— Yes. Then the prep school went out within a year or two. They closed it out because they thought the state then had high schools that everybody could have a chance for high school.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you tell the story about your wanting to go to college and your father's— Oh, well, I—when I was in the year I was in prep school, the physical education teacher said, why don't you figure on—this is a coming thing, physical education in the public school—why don't you go on with that?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, the university, it was just—everybody was required to take physical education, but it wasn't much more than calisthenics. I don't think we even played volleyball or basketball or anything. It was just mostly in folk dancing, calisthenics and folk dancing. So the Lewis to Normal offered a course in physical education. I want you to tell the story about enrolling at the U of I. Oh, oh, yes. Your father, thinking that there were only certain majors. Well, yes, my father was of the old school, and he thought you should either be a teacher—and finally he came to being a secretary was all right, too—or stay at home. And he was always a great admirer of fancy work, we called it, hand work and so on. He thought it was wonderful when young girls just could be satisfied to do that sort of thing. Well, I was in that disposition. So I did start the college, registered and went to college, and I took home economics, which my father thought was a very fine thing. That was the home economics was before they were even in the administration building. That was one thing they did in the administration building when they got rid of prep school and they put the home economics later on up there. Of course, it was a law school for a while, too, in the third floor. I registered in home economics, and the first assignment in home economics over in Ridenbaugh Hall where they had set up this temporary home economics department was the canned fruit for Ridenbaugh Hall. It was, of course, fall and the pears were on, and we were the canned pears. Well, we had to wash the jars, and so everybody took their jars, old mason fruit jars, to the sink to wash them. I ran hot water on mine and broke it. Of course, that wasn't the thing I was supposed to do. The teacher, I don't know that she said too much, but she explained that wasn't the way to wash fruit jars, just running hot water over them and so on. I didn't last very long that year, because I just knew I wasn't going to—wasn't the right temperament for that sort of thing. So I didn't finish the year. Then the next year, my father let me go down to Lewiston Normal to take my physical education. It happened that the head of the department in Lewiston was Miss Bacher from New York, who was teaching to earn money to go on through medical school. I liked her very much, and I liked what she taught us. I just thoroughly enjoyed her, and that just made me want to be a doctor. We had to take so many courses that were so foolish for young people to take. We took the same anatomy that they took at medical school and kinesiology. She had a year or two of medical school, you see. As I remember, she didn't teach any of the floor work, as we called it, in physical education. She just taught the book subjects that went along with the course. We just adored her. We had a skeleton that hung with a screw up in a box case. We knew physiology, and we had anatomy and bacteriology. The bacteriology was over in the home economics building, because all girls in home economics at Lewiston Normal had to take the course in bacteriology, too. But after I was through and I wanted to go on to medical school, my father—no, no daughter of his would go to medical school. That was the profession that women should be in. That was a man's profession. I just was at home then. I told you my experience of teaching in the Kendrick schools.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Kelly, did that bother you when your father said that?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Litzy, and my brother was the same way with my niece. She wanted to be a nurse, and she would have been a wonderful nurse. They lived back in Chicago. But she went to Beloit, but he wouldn't think of her going to study nursing.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That just wasn't done in their way of thinking.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You want anything else?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We got to get our breakfast pretty soon. We could finish the date.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was it as a young woman after you came back from Lewiston? What things did you do?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I tell you, there really wasn't too much in the way of social life. There was quite a little group of us who were not in school, and we'd get up and dance occasionally.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But then the flu years—we had soldiers at the university in ROTC. Well, it was just a general. It wasn't all ROTC. They were sent here to be trained. The flu epidemic came on, and I worked in Red Cross then, because I was needed. Other young women in town who weren't in school worked in Red Cross. There were so many ill people among these soldiers that they had them in many places in just temporary hospitals, you see. They took buildings in town.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: There had to be bedding provided and pillows and sheets and everything. It just seemed to me every week I had a certain amount of solicitation among the residents of Moscow to provide something. But people were so good because the necessity was so great. Then some of the young women who—they had a place where they prepared meals for all of these out buildings that were hospitals.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: The food had to be taken. I was driving at that time. My father got in the car and he didn't learn to drive it, but I drove it. I often was called upon to drive. That was a very difficult winter here in Moscow because of so much flu. Harry was in the store then at Davidson. He would measure a man. He ordered all the uniforms and maybe the young fellow would be dead before the uniform ever came. There were so many deaths. It was very sad.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But that's the way I occupied my time until I was married.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you help a lot of women, too?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, yes. Yes, I did. I helped with the housework. I didn't learn to cook, but I did other things because my sister, Myra, was home. My mother died in 1918. Of course, she hadn't been doing the housework anyway. But we both did.
